paper.title: Machine learning aided construction of the quorum sensing communication network for human gut microbiota

proof_flows.name: The three-module workflow integrates collected, expanded, and mined datasets into the QSHGM resource for human gut microbes.

items.verify: A workflow summary artifact exists and records the major datasets or stage outputs used to build QSHGM.
items.expected: A non-empty JSON/CSV/markdown summary is produced for the collecting, expanding, and mining workflow, with usable dataset-count fields or tables.

items.verify: The reproduced workflow yields the same dataset sizes reported for the ten named datasets.
items.expected: Dataset sizes match the paper: Dataset I = 213, Dataset II = 818, Dataset III = 21,383, Dataset IV = 22,780, Dataset V = 14,573, Dataset VI = 5,320, Dataset VII = 9,253, Dataset VIII = 534, Dataset IX = 7,184, Dataset X = 28,567.

items.verify: The reproduced workflow preserves the same high-level architecture and classifier usage as the paper.
items.expected: The workflow is organized into collecting, expanding, and mining modules, and the expanding stage uses four classifiers: DNN, SVM, KNN, and RF, whose outputs are combined before manual/functional mining into QSHGM.

proof_flows.name: The reported and annotated QS entries show dominant AHL synthases, dominant LuxR/TCS receptors, and a strongly skewed distribution of QS&TCS entries across gut microbes.

items.verify: Phylogenetic tree artifacts and reported-entry summary tables are generated for the curated reported QS/QS&TCS entries.
items.expected: Non-empty tree/image and count-summary outputs are produced for synthases, receptors, annotation counts, overlap analysis, and strain-level distributions.

items.verify: The curated validated QS dataset contains the same numbers of synthases and receptors as reported.
items.expected: Dataset I contains 84 autoinducer synthases and 129 QS receptors.

items.verify: The phylogenetic trees reproduce the same broad composition and interpretation as the paper.
items.expected: The synthase tree is dominated by AHL synthases, the receptor tree is dominated by LuxR-type and TCS receptors, and the AHL synthase and receptor trees show broadly similar structure consistent with the paper's coevolution claim.

items.verify: The reproduced annotation counts for the manually collected QS&TCS entries match the four categories in the paper.
items.expected: Counts by annotation are: quorum sensing = 1,640; LuxR = 5,921; two-component = 15,703; tryptophanase = 66.

items.verify: The seven-strain distribution panel shows the same dominant classes as described in the paper.
items.expected: Across the selected SIHUMI strains, LuxR-type and two-component entries dominate the stacked distributions, with quorum-sensing and tryptophanase entries much smaller.

items.verify: The overlap analysis for Escherichia coli O157:H7 recovers the same shared LuxR/TCS entries reported in the paper.
items.expected: Exactly 7 entries are shared by LuxR-type and two-component annotations in E. coli O157:H7: P69409, P0ACZ6, P0AGA8, P66798, P0AF30, P0AEL9, and Q8XE66.

items.verify: The strain-level distribution of collected QS&TCS entries matches the skew reported in the paper.
items.expected: The cumulative distribution shows about 90% of strains contain fewer than 60 QS&TCS entries, and only 7 strains contain more than 150 entries.
items.tolerance: ±2 percentage points for the 90% statement; ±0 for the count of 7 strains

proof_flows.name: AAC-based machine learning plus manual mining expands the QS repository, re-annotates uncharacterized proteins, and increases QSHGM coverage.

items.verify: Classifier metrics, union-of-positives summaries, cluster summaries, and re-annotation tables for expanded entries are produced.
items.expected: Non-empty metric files and output tables/images exist for classifier evaluation, predicted positives, cluster assignments, final expanded-entry summary, BLASTP-based re-annotations, and Phyre2-based re-annotations.

items.verify: The reproduced fivefold cross-validation metrics for the four classifiers agree with the values labeled in the paper.
items.expected: Classifier metrics (from figure labels) are: DNN accuracy 0.823, precision 0.799, recall 0.820, F1 0.809; SVM accuracy 0.829, precision 0.798, recall 0.842, F1 0.819; KNN accuracy 0.864, precision 0.799, recall 0.943, F1 0.865; RF accuracy 0.896, precision 0.903, recall 0.869, F1 0.886.

items.verify: The classifier outputs contain the same counts of annotated and uncharacterized positives as shown in the paper.
items.expected: Annotated/uncharacterized positives are DNN 4,138/223, SVM 5,280/309, KNN 4,805/294, and RF 4,620/307.

items.verify: The cluster breakdown of the 534 re-annotated uncharacterized entries matches the paper.
items.expected: Cluster counts are: Acyl-CoA dehydrogenase 42; Enoyl-CoA hydratase 29; Histidine kinase 163; HTH domain-containing 106; OmpR family 48; Sigma 54-dependent 48; Others 9; Not QS 61; No annotation 28.

items.verify: The final expanded repository size and overall QSHGM growth match the paper.
items.expected: The final database contains 28,567 redundancy-removed entries for 818 gut microbes, including 7,184 extended entries; this is a 33.60% increase over the original 21,383 reported entries. The full set is composed of 1,882 QS synthases and 26,685 receptors.

items.verify: The BLASTP re-annotation table contains the same number of entries as the paper.
items.expected: Table 1 contains 20 expanded entries re-annotated using web BLASTP.

items.verify: Representative Table 1 re-annotations match the paper's biological interpretation.
items.expected: The re-annotation table includes labels such as ArsR subfamily regulator, Autoinducer 2 ABC transporter, Beta-ketoacyl-ACP synthase III, Histidine kinase / Sensor histidine kinase, LuxR family regulator, Rgg/GadR/MutR family regulator, and Tryptophanase for the corresponding listed accessions.

items.verify: The Phyre2 re-annotation table contains the same number of entries as the paper.
items.expected: Table 2 contains 8 expanded entries re-annotated using Phyre2.

items.verify: The Phyre2-based annotation pattern agrees with the paper.
items.expected: Most Phyre2-based re-annotations correspond to AimR transcriptional regulator templates, with the remaining entries matching a signaling protein (tetratricopeptide repeat) and an AgrC ATP-binding domain.

proof_flows.name: The QSHGM resource can be queried to predict specific QS-language-mediated communications in example microbial consortia.

items.verify: Example consortium interaction diagrams or edge lists are generated for the paper's named strain sets.
items.expected: Non-empty network images or edge tables are produced for the example strain combinations used to illustrate predicted QS-based communication.

items.verify: The simplest example reproduces the same pairwise prediction as the paper.
items.expected: Escherichia coli O157:H7 and Bacteroides pectinophilus ATCC 43243 are connected through AI-2 only.

items.verify: The three-microbe example reproduces the same added language and strain relationship as the paper.
items.expected: Enterobacteriaceae bacterium 9_2_54FAA adds indole-mediated communication while the AI-2 link between Escherichia coli O157:H7 and Bacteroides pectinophilus ATCC 43243 remains present.

items.verify: The four-microbe example reproduces the same enrichment of communication modes as the paper.
items.expected: Adding Pseudomonas aeruginosa PAO1 introduces AHL-mediated links on top of the AI-2 and indole interactions already present.

items.verify: The five-microbe example reproduces the same multi-language network as the paper.
items.expected: Adding Burkholderia cepacia GG4 introduces HAQs and DSFs, yielding a consortium network that uses AI-2, indole, AHLs, HAQs, and DSFs.

proof_flows.name: The constructed QSCN shows broad QS-language usage across 818 gut microbes, substantial language sharing, and heterogeneous taxonomic distribution at genus level.

items.verify: Bipartite QSCN outputs, overlap analyses, and genus-level clustering artifacts are generated.
items.expected: Non-empty network/heatmap outputs and corresponding summary tables are produced for the 818-microbe bipartite QSCN, the language-overlap analysis, and the genus-level clustering view.

items.verify: The counts of gut microbes speaking each QS language match the paper.
items.expected: Language counts across 818 gut microbes are: AI-2 567, HAQs 332, DSFs 325, CAI-1 259, Dialkylresorcinols 129, Photopyrones 107, indole 77, AHLs 64, and AIPs 22.

items.verify: The overlap summary for typical languages matches the paper.
items.expected: The overlap analysis reports 64 species sharing two languages, 40 sharing three, 22 sharing four, and 5 sharing five; AI-2 has the highest genus-level count at 138.

items.verify: The genus-level clustering panel is built at the same scale reported in the paper.
items.expected: The hierarchical clustering covers 210 genus-level taxa and organizes them into 10 genus-level clusters.

items.verify: The reproduced genus-level distribution supports the same biological interpretation as the paper.
items.expected: AHLs appear only in Proteobacteria, AIPs are concentrated mainly in Firmicutes, the other languages are distributed heterogeneously, and closely related genera do not show a strongly conserved shared language profile.

proof_flows.name: A directed small QSCN for the SIHUMI community separates signal production from reception and suggests that some microbes receive signals they do not produce.

items.verify: A directed small-network artifact is produced for the SIHUMI example with separate producing and receiving sides.
items.expected: A non-empty directed network image or edge table exists that distinguishes QS signal producing from QS signal receiving for the SIHUMI example community.

items.verify: The reproduced small QSCN supports the same interpretation as the paper.
items.expected: The small QSCN separates signal production and reception for the listed SIHUMI microbes, with the receiving side visibly denser/more complex than the producing side and some microbes connected on the receiving side without mirrored producing links.
